content_en:
Minhaj as-Sunnah has generated controversy and scholarly debate across the centuries since its composition. It is a work that both its admirers and its critics recognize as a scholarly achievement of the first order — detailed, rigorous, and drawing on an extraordinary breadth of sources — while disagreeing fundamentally about whether its conclusions are sound.

Within the Sunni tradition, Minhaj as-Sunnah has been received differently across different periods and tendencies. For scholars in the Hanbali and Athari traditions, it represents the most comprehensive Sunni engagement with Shia theology and a definitive articulation of the Sunni position on the companions and early Islamic history. For scholars in other Sunni schools, the work's value is recognized while some of its positions are seen as going beyond what the evidence requires or as failing to distinguish sufficiently between different Shia groups and different periods of Shia thought.

Shia scholars have naturally engaged with the work critically. Classical Shia responses were composed in the decades following Ibn Taymiyyah's death. Modern Shia scholarship has continued to engage with the arguments, often acknowledging the sophistication of Ibn Taymiyyah's method while disputing his handling of specific texts and his characterization of Shia doctrines. Some modern scholars, both Sunni and Shia, have used the work as a point of departure for more nuanced historical analysis of the early Islamic period, finding that Ibn Taymiyyah's questions were the right ones even where his answers are debated.

Western academic scholarship on Ibn Taymiyyah has grown substantially in recent decades. Scholars such as Henri Laoust, Yahya Michot, Jon Hoover, and Caterina Bori have examined different aspects of his thought, including Minhaj as-Sunnah, and have situated it within broader questions about the development of Sunni and Shia identities and the history of Islamic political theology. This academic engagement has revealed the work's complexity and resisted both hagiographic and purely polemical readings.

One dimension of the work that receives sustained attention is its historical methodology. Ibn Taymiyyah applies what might be called a proto-critical approach to the sources: he examines chains of transmission, notes inconsistencies between accounts, and distinguishes between what can be reliably established and what is speculation or partisan construction. This methodology anticipates some features of modern historical criticism, even though it operates within a very different framework of assumptions about authority and evidence.

Minhaj as-Sunnah remains a living text in contemporary Islamic discourse. It is studied in traditional Islamic educational institutions in the Sunni world as a foundational text on creed and the understanding of early Islamic history. It is engaged with polemically in Sunni-Shia controversies that have intensified in the contemporary period. And it is studied academically as one of the most revealing documents in the long history of Islamic sectarian formation and theological debate.
